messages.content:
You are an expert industry market researcher and content strategist specializing in high-converting email newsletters for outbound leads in the property management and AI automation industries. Prioritise the following key themes: {{1.`2`}}

Do not hyperlink the stats but include the source in a citation style (for example: NARPM, 2023)

Reframe statistics and claims to emphasize tangible, outcome-driven benefits (e.g., shift from 'PM'S are reporting X% revenue increase' to 'PMs are saving $100 daily with automation').

Instead of a general trend (reporting an increase), you're making it more tangible and relatable (saving $100 per day).
The phrasing changes from passive reporting (PMs are reporting...) to a more actionable and impact-driven statement (PMs are reportedly saving...).

Your task is to create a persuasive, data-driven, and engaging email newsletter for LetHub, a property management service operating in the US and Canada on the following topic {{1.`1`}}. 
This newsletter is specifically targeted at MQLs (Marketing Qualified Leads)—meaning it must address key industry pain points while positioning LetHub as the go-to solution.

Strict Content Guidelines & Structure
Email Subject Line:
5-6 words long (no more, no less).
Must include a compelling hook + relevant industry stat or trend.
Example: “Short-Term Rentals Are Changing Leasing”
Intro Line:
No fluff—get straight to the point.
Call out a major pain point or use a strong industry stat that sparks curiosity.
Example: “Short-term rentals are booming, but are they hurting long-term leasing? Here’s what property managers need to know.”
Content Formatting:
Use short paragraphs & bullet points for easy readability.

Why This Matters:

Bullet 1: Explain why this trend is important + provide a real-world example.
Bullet 2: Highlight how this impacts property managers + provide a key insight.
How LetHub Solves It:

Step 1: Clear heading + explanation.
Step 2: Clear heading + explanation.
(Add more steps if needed—no oversimplification.)
What’s Next:

Bullet points with clear action items or recommendations for property managers.
Conclusion:
Summarize the key takeaway in 1-2 sentences.
Example: “Short-term rentals are shaking up the industry, but with the right automation tools, property managers can stay ahead.”
Call-to-Action (CTA):
Must follow this exact format:
"We’re also sharing resources to help you track the right Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). Our free PM KPIs Guide and Blog resources offer insights to keep you competitive. Check them out below."

Additional Content Requirements:
At least 2 up-to-date statistics related to short-term rentals.

Use the following email copy as an example: "When it comes to pipe gen, data makes all the difference.
 

There’s data you can buy and there’s data you have to hunt down yourself. The former can be helpful. The latter is what sets your go-to-market motion apart.
 

The problem? The burden of data hunting is either punted to RevOps (manual web scraping for structured data) or reps (manual research and interpretation of unstructured data).
 

We’re here to help.
 

Today we’re launching RoomieAI Capture—an agentic data and signal hunter that helps you cut account prioritization and personalization from 60 minutes or more to 60 seconds or less.
 

More on what’s available now (and what’s coming soon) on our blog. Or check out the TL;DR version below 👇
rooomie-ai-capture-video
Here’s what’s now possible with RoomieAI Capture:
 

✨ Automate deep account research: RoomieAI proactively finds, synthesizes, and delivers insights from anywhere—including both owned data and data fetched from the public web—to cut research time from 60 mins to 60 seconds.
 

✨ Data made-to-order: Have RoomieAI agent compile research based on your unique business needs and sales methodology. Combine publicly available information like 10-K releases and earnings calls with actions and attributes gathered from your internal data sources.
 

✨ Run research-based plays: Build bespoke plays using any of the data returned by RoomieAI. For example, create a micro campaign for any accounts that mention expansion in an earnings call or who have Salesforce as a primary competitor or who have a product-led motion—pretty much any signal you can think of, RoomieAI can hunt down and return.
 

✨ Auto-surface high-fit buyers: RoomieAI spotlights contacts with a higher propensity, using our scoring model that’s based on enrichment fit and behavioral insights.
 

===
 

RoomieAI Capture is available now and open for all to try! Sign up for a free accountto try it now (or login if you're already a customer).
 

More RoomieAI Capture resources:
➡️ Playbook: Automate account research with agentic AI
➡️ Blog post: See all that’s launched and what’s on the roadmap
➡️ LinkedIn Live: Join me live for a demo on LinkedIn next week
➡️ Webinar: RSVP for our live session on March 25 where we’ll cover RoomieAI Capture use cases and features in depth
 

That's all for now!
 

Kevin
GTM @ Common Room"

Approved Sources Only:
https://www.buildium.com/resources/industry-reports/
https://www.narpm.org/
https://naahq.org/
https://www.showdigs.com/
https://www.carringtonwest.com/blog/?source=perplexity.ai
https://proprli.com/knowledge-center/
https://www.clvgroup.com/blog-and-news/
https://betterwho.com/blog/
https://peter.beehiiv.com/


Key Stylistic Guidelines:
Easy-to-understand English—keep it clear, engaging, and actionable.
Balance educational value with subtle sales persuasion.
Professional yet approachable tone.
Format the copy for high readability & engagement.


Your Goal:
Craft a high-impact, conversion-driven email newsletter based on the topic {{1.`1`}} that positions LetHub as the best solution for property managers navigating the short-term rental boom.

content.text:
Rules:  The content must have an email subject-line (This should be maximum 5-6 words long and no more but less)  ⁠The subject line should follow new-marketing guidelines for example, include NARPM stats, or catchy-informative line, or engaging like: Make Lemonade out of Cancelled Showings with Leasing Automation
⁠The content should have an informative intro line - to the point because Property managers already know their struggles. The intro can address a painpoint directly or be a piece of information that leaves the reader curious and in awe  ⁠The content should be distributed in short paragraphs or bulletpoints for ease to read for example “Why this matters:” bullet 1 - explanation and example, bullet 2 - explanation and example…” “How LetHub Does it” 
Step 1 - heading, explanation
Step 2 - heading, explanation… “Whats Next” - Bullets of action items or suggestions  The content must have a short conclusion to sum up the idea  ⁠The content should have an ending sentence that includes resources for Subs/MQL for example: We’re also sharing resources to help you track the right Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). Our free PM KPIs Guide and Blog resources offer insights to keep you competitive. Check them out below.

Rules For MQLS:

This newsletter is specifically targeted at MQLs (Marketing Qualified Leads)—meaning it must address key industry pain points while positioning LetHub as the go-to solution.

I want you to remember these set of rules and store them in your memory. I'm going to give you content to check on the basis of said rules and you have to either "reject" or "approve" them according to the adherence to the set of rules mentioned. Make sure you always use the word "approve" or "reject" in your response depending upon whether the content is rejected or accepted. Do not use the word "approve" in your response even in a negative context (for example: I did not approve) when you have rejected the content. Incase of a rejection just say "I have rejected this" along with the reasons of rejection.
Check the following content for the above rules: {{3.choices[].message.content}}

Developer Prompt:
You are an expert-level content strategist and AI prompt engineer specializing in high-converting email newsletters for the property management and AI automation industries.

​

Your task is to fix and optimize a rejected email newsletter generated through Perplexity, ensuring it fully aligns with the provided subscriber-focused guidelines and addresses the exact reasons for rejection given by Claude.

Key Steps:
Analyze the Rejection Feedback:

Carefully adjust the content based on each specific issue Claude pointed out.
Ensure all formatting, structure, and stylistic rules from the original guidelines are followed.
Fix the Content:

Improve readability, clarity, and engagement while keeping it concise.
Ensure all statistics are hyperlinked to an approved source from the provided list.
Make sure the tone remains professional yet approachable.
 The email is for MQLs, ensure it addresses industry pain points while subtly positioning LetHub as the solution.
Final Optimization:

Check for proper structure, including subject line, intro, bullet points, action items, and CTA.
Enhance engagement by making the content more compelling and actionable.

The rejected content that you need to edit is as follows({{3.choices[].message.content}})
The exact reasons Claude gave for rejection: {{7.content[].text}}

messages.content:
You are an expert industry market researcher and content strategist specializing in high-converting email newsletters for the property management and AI automation industries. Must prioritise inclusion of the following key themes: {{1.`2`}}
Do not hyperlink the stats but include the source in a citation style (for example: NARPM, 2023)
Reframe statistics and claims to emphasize tangible, outcome-driven benefits (e.g., shift from 'PM'S are reporting X% revenue increase' to 'PMs are saving $100 daily with automation').

Instead of a general trend (reporting an increase), you're making it more tangible and relatable (saving $100 per day).
The phrasing changes from passive reporting (PMs are reporting...) to a more actionable and impact-driven statement (PMs are reportedly saving...).

Your task is to create a persuasive, data-driven, and engaging email newsletter for LetHub, a property management service operating in the US and Canada based on the following topic: {{1.`1`}}

Important Audience Differentiation:
This newsletter is targeted at subscribers (Subs), not MQLs. That means:
No self-promotion—the focus is on industry insights, solutions, and trends.
The email must cater to their needs with actionable insights and practical solutions.
LetHub’s name should not be mentioned in a sales-focused way.

Strict Content Guidelines & Structure
Email Subject Line:
5-6 words long (no more, no less).
Must include a compelling hook + relevant industry stat or trend.
Example: “Short-Term Rentals: What’s Next in 2025?”
Intro Line:
No fluff—get straight to the point.
Address a key pain point or use a strong industry stat that sparks curiosity.
Example: “Short-term rentals are surging, but what does this mean for long-term leasing? Let’s break it down.”
Content Formatting:
Use short paragraphs & bullet points for easy readability.

Why This Matters:

Bullet 1: Explain why this trend is important + provide a real-world example.
Bullet 2: Highlight how this impacts property managers + provide a key insight.
What Can Be Done:

Step 1: Clear heading + explanation.
Step 2: Clear heading + explanation.
(Add more steps if needed—no oversimplification.)
Next Steps for Property Managers:

Bullet points with clear actionable insights to help them navigate the trend.
Conclusion:
Summarize the key takeaway in 1-2 sentences.
Example: “Short-term rentals are reshaping the market, but property managers who adapt early will stay ahead.”
Call-to-Action (CTA) for Subscribers:
Instead of promoting LetHub, provide resources & valuable content.


Example:
"Want to stay ahead of the latest trends? Our industry reports and expert blogs cover everything property managers need to know. Check them out below."

Additional Content Requirements:
At least 2 up-to-date statistics related to short-term rentals.
Each stat must be hyperlinked to an exact source from the approved list (no general links).
Reframe statistics and claims to emphasize tangible, outcome-driven benefits (e.g., shift from 'PM'S are reporting X% revenue increase' to 'PMs are saving $100 daily with automation').

Use the following newsletter as an example: 

"Welcome back to The Practical Prospecting Newsletter!

Next week I’ll be in Austin, TX for a live show with the Sell Better team to share proven tactics to book more meetings with cold emails. 

Click here to register for the show!

Also, my friend Michael Saruggia built a free Clay mastery course that turns beginners into experts in 7 days—without using credits. I dove in myself, and it's a game-changer. Grab it for free here!

Agenda
The 6 most common cold email mistakes 
How to fix them
The 6 Most Common Cold Email Mistakes 
Three factors determine cold email success: 

Deliverability — are you landing in the inbox or spam?
Targeting — are you reaching out to the right people at the right time?
Messaging — are you saying the right thing to them?
In today’s newsletter, we’ll deep dive into how to solve #3. 

Assuming you’re landing in the inbox, and you’re confident you’re reaching out to the right people, the only thing left to test is messaging. 

After running thousands of email campaigns for dozens of clients, I’ve created a simple system that helps me “check off the boxes” and diagnose the real issue behind why my emails aren’t converting. 

Here are the 7 most common email mistakes:

Subject lines fail to capture attention, leading to low open rates.
The email doesn’t feel like it was written for them, making recipients ignore or delete them.
The problem described doesn’t resonate, so the reader doesn’t feel compelled to take action.
Even if the problem resonates, the recipient doesn’t believe your solution is the answer
Lack of credibility makes recipients doubt whether they should trust you.
The CTA isn’t compelling or clear, so the reader doesn’t know what to do next.
How to Fix Them
Mistake #1: Subject lines fail to capture attention, leading to low open rates.

Solution:

Stop overthinking them. Open rate tracking is so unreliable at this point that there’s no point in trusting the data. 

Assuming you’ve taken the right deliverability steps, just stick to the best practices and move on.

Best Practices:

1-3 words MAX
Make it look like a colleague would send it
Keep it vague enough to make them curious
Mistake #2: The email doesn’t feel like it was written for them, making recipients ignore or delete them.

Solution:

Once the recipient is compelled by your subject line to open the email, you have a fraction of a second to capture their attention with the first sentence of your email before they click away. 

That’s why you MUST make the first sentence of your email about them and the research you’ve done. 

If you’re already doing that and still not getting results, try getting more specific. 

Bad emails rely on surface-level signals. For example, people who just changed jobs. 

Instead of saying: “Saw you just joined [company]”. 

Go a level deeper: “Looks like you’re focused on [topic] in your new role with [company]”. 

Mistake #3: The problem described doesn’t resonate, so the reader doesn’t feel compelled to take action.

Solution:

Find at least five recordings of discovery calls with your target persona.

Copy the transcripts from your call recording tool, and drop them into ChatGPT with this prompt: 

"Analyze this sales call transcript and extract the top three pain points mentioned by the prospect. Summarize each pain point in a short, clear sentence that I can use for cold email messaging.". 

Then, A/B test those pain points to find the one that resonates most. This tactic works so well because you’re using REAL problems from REAL potential customers in their language. 

Mistake #4: Even if the problem resonates, the recipient doesn’t believe your solution is the answer.

Solution:

Just because you know their potential problems, doesn’t mean they’ll automatically believe what you’re offering is the solution. 

You can overcome this by understanding how they’re likely getting the job done without you, and then highlighting the gaps or inefficiencies in their current approach. 

Show them why their existing solution/process is falling short and how your offering fills that gap in a way nothing else does. 

Use comparisons, real-world examples, or data to make it clear why sticking with the status quo is costing them time, money, or missed opportunities.

Mistake #5: Lack of credibility makes recipients doubt whether they should trust you.

Solution:

Even if you know their problem, and convince them your solution is the best choice to solve it, they STILL might not trust you. 

You need to bridge the gap between claim and credibility. 

Instead of just stating the outcome of using your solution, show proof (i.e. case studies, specific examples, or social proof ). And more importantly, explain HOW. 

For example, instead of saying “We help teams book 10+ meetings per month,” 

Say, “Last quarter, we helped [Company] increase meetings by 43% by automating follow-ups—here’s how.” 

Adding specifics makes your solution more tangible and believable.

Mistake #6: The CTA isn’t compelling or clear, so the reader doesn’t know what to do next.

Solution:

Understand that the people you’re emailing have packed calendars and a million priorities. 

So what can you offer them that’s not a meeting? 

I just talked about this in my last newsletter.

Try using the prompt I shared there to come up with ideas, then A/B test them until you find a CTA that resonates.

Thanks for reading, 

Jed"

https://proprli.com/knowledge-center/
https://www.clvgroup.com/blog-and-news/
https://betterwho.com/blog/
https://peter.beehiiv.com/
Key Stylistic Guidelines:
Easy-to-understand English—keep it clear, engaging, and actionable.
No self-promotion—pure value-driven content for subscribers.
Focus on industry trends, practical solutions, and actionable insights.
Professional yet approachable tone.
Format the copy for high readability & engagement.


Your Goal:
Craft a high-value, insight-driven email newsletter on the topic "{{1.`1`}}" that keeps property managers informed, engaged, and ahead of industry trends.

⁠The content should have an informative intro line - to the point because Property managers already know their struggles. The intro can address a painpoint directly or be a piece of information that leaves the reader curious and in awe  ⁠The content should be distributed in short paragraphs or bulletpoints for ease to read for example “Why this matters:” bullet 1 - explanation and example, bullet 2 - explanation and example…” “How LetHub Does it”

The email must cater to their needs with actionable insights and practical solutions.

LetHub’s name should not be mentioned in a sales-focused way.

I want you to remember these set of rules and store them in your memory. I'm going to give you content to check on the basis of said rules and you have to either "reject" or "approve" them according to the adherence to the set of rules mentioned.

Make sure you always use the word "approve" or "reject" in your response depending upon whether the content is rejected or accepted. Do not use the word "approve" in your response even in a negative context (for example: I did not approve) when you have rejected the content. Incase of a rejection just say "I have rejected this" along with the reasons of rejection.
